source.title: Ultrahigh-throughput screening-assisted in vivo directed evolution for enzyme engineering.

initial_request.research_problem: Classical directed evolution is labor- and time-intensive due to manual steps and limited screening throughput.
initial_request.known_context: Classical directed evolution methods rely on manual operation for rounds of stepwise processes, including DNA extraction, in vitro mutagenesis, transformation and selection, which are usually labour intensive and limit the efficiency, scale and depth of evolution.
constraints: Must enable continuous in vivo evolution
quantifiable_goals: Increase mutation rate over wild-type
Achieve >20% activity improvement in target enzymes
Enable FACS-based screening

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to evolve a tighter repressor to achieve better regulation.

metrics: Percentage of cells with >20% activity increase
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ultrahigh-throughput screening-assisted in vivo directed evolution system successfully evolved α-amylase (N473Y, 48.3% improvement) and resveratrol pathway (1.7-fold improvement via plasmid backbone mutations), but limitations include low mutation rate, off-target genomic mutations, and non-coding mutations masking protein improvements.
limitations: Low mutation rate (6.77×10⁻⁸ per base per generation) limits sequence space exploration.
Off-target genomic mutations require plasmid retransformation between rounds.
Beneficial mutations may occur in non-coding regions rather than protein-coding sequences.

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution (DE) is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
initial_request.target_object: ParPgb protoglobin for non-native cyclopropanation reaction
initial_request.known_context: DE can be inefficient when mutations exhibit non-additive, or epistatic, behavior. DE is limited because screening for performance can only explore a small, local region of sequence space. Taking one mutational step at a time can cause the experiment to become stuck at a local optimum, especially on rugged protein fitness landscapes where mutation effects exhibit epistasis.
constraints: Combinatorial design space on five active-site residues (W56, Y57, L59, Q60, F89)
Objective: high total cyclopropanation yield and cis:trans selectivity for cis-2a product
input_data: Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) data at five positions
Recombination data of best single mutants (DAYFW, DGMDW, DGMVW)
quantifiable_goals: Improve total cyclopropanation yield beyond 12% (parent ParLQ)
Achieve high cis:trans selectivity for cis-2a

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DE is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to explore protein sequence space more efficiently than standard DE when epistasis is present.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Active learning with uncertainty quantification can explore protein sequence space more efficiently than DE.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop Active Learning-assisted Directed Evolution (ALDE) workflow.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Active learning has the potential to unlock improved engineering outcomes by leveraging uncertainty quantification to choose which variants should be tested at each step.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the ALDE workflow to test the hypothesis.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Workflow steps: simultaneously mutate k residues, collect initial sequence-fitness data, train supervised ML model, apply acquisition function to rank sequences, repeat cycle until fitness optimized.

evidence.quote_or_span: DNN ensembles have the lowest miscalibration areas, suggesting that they are the most calibrated and best models overall.
However, in this study, we find that performance in ALDE simulations (by max fitness achieved) is not necessarily correlated to how calibrated the uncertainties are for each model.
Overall, classical notions of uncertainty quantification seem to play a more limited role than expected in these real-world applications.

validation_methods: Wet-lab screening of Round 1 and Round 2 variants

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALDE successfully improved total yield from 12% to 99% and cis:trans selectivity to 14:1 after exploring only ~0.01% of the design space. Simulations confirm ALDE outperforms average DE and random sampling.
limitations: Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.

source.title: selection finder selfi a computational metabolic engineering tool to enable dire

initial_request.research_problem: Directed enzyme evolution is bottlenecked by the lack of high-throughput selections to isolate desired variants.
initial_request.target_object: Selection pathways coupling enzymatic product to host survival
initial_request.known_context: Most directed evolution is limited by the inability to generically link any biochemical transformation to readouts like cell density or fluorescence.
constraints: Maximum 3 knockouts to limit computational cost
input_data: E. coli iAF1260 genome-scale metabolic model
quantifiable_goals: Guarantee minimum flux through consumption pathway
Rank pathways by flux, length, and number of knockouts

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed enzyme evolution lacks generic high-throughput selections.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No computational framework exists to systematically construct selection pathways linking any enzymatic product to host survival.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational metabolic engineering framework can construct selection pathways from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and couple the pathway with cell survival.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop SelFi, a four-step computational framework.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi is designed to identify selection pathways in four steps: construct consumption pathways, eliminate alternate carbon sources, identify knockout targets, rank pathways.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to detail the four steps of SelFi.

evidence.quote_or_span: The biggest challenge in directed enzyme evolution is identifying high-throughput screens or selections to isolate the variant(s) with the desired property.
most directed evolution of enzymes are still largely limited by the inability to identify and implement selections or screens.

We present in this paper a computational metabolic engineering framework, Selection Finder (SelFi), to construct a selection pathway from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and to couple the pathway with cell survival.

SelFi identifies a selection pathway in four steps. In the first step, SelFi constructs traversal pathways to consume the desired enzymatic reaction product and convert it to a native metabolite within the cellular host.

SelFi continues to find an additional knockout target that improves the flux, repeating this process until the maximum allowed number of knockouts, as specified by the user, is reached. initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid production under low-pH conditions to reduce neutralization costs and enable use of cheap feedstocks.
initial_request.known_context: Current lactic acid production via LAB requires large amounts of neutralization agents and complex nutrients, limiting sustainability. S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
quantifiable_goals: High lactic acid titer
High yield
Low-pH production

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactic acid production by LAB requires neutralization and complex nutrients; S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a robust, acid-tolerant, Crabtree-negative host for low-pH lactic acid production.

source.title: A machine learning approach for reliable prediction of amino acid interactions and its application in the directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by epistasis, where non-additive effects of mutations impair prediction of improved variants. The goal is to develop a machine learning method that predicts amino acid interactions from sequence and fitness data alone, without requiring 3D structure, to guide the engineering of enantioselective enzymes.
initial_request.known_context: Epistasis impairs predictions in protein engineering; machine learning has not been applied to evolve enzyme mutants with enhanced or inverted stereoselectivity.

initial_request.research_problem: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.
initial_request.known_context: Traditional directed evolution methods are low-throughput and often miss epistatic interactions. Selection methods focus on single transformations, limiting the collection of positive and negative sequence-function relationships.

source.title: Accelerated directed evolution of dye-decolorizing peroxidase using a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY)

initial_request.research_problem: Enhance H2O2 tolerance of dye-decolorizing peroxidase 4 (DyP4) from Pleurotus ostreatus using directed evolution, accelerated by a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY).
initial_request.known_context: DyP4 is a haem peroxidase with potential for lignin valorization but is sensitive to H2O2 inactivation. Methionine oxidation was hypothesized as a cause.
constraints: Must maintain proper folding and activity
input_data: Crystal structure of DyP4 F194Y variant (PDB 6FSK)
quantifiable_goals: Improve H2O2 tolerance (IC50)
Maintain or improve catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Create methionine-substituted variants (M43L, M77L, M253L, M253F) to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: In an attempt to enhance the $H_{2}O_{2}$ tolerance of DyP4, we created 4 methionine-substituted variants (M43L M77L M253L and M253F).

To construct M43L (primers M43L-F and M43L-R), M253L (primers M253L-F and M253L-R) and M253F (primers M253F-F and M253F-R) variants, partially overlapping primers and Q5 high-fidelity DNA polymerase (New England Biolabs) were used in a 2-stage PCR.
To construct M77L variant (primers M77L-F and M77L-R), DNA polymerase used was substituted with PfuUltra high-fidelity DNA polymerase AD (Agilent Technologies).
All methionine-substituted variants were created using pET-24a(+)-DyP4 as template.

The conversion performed by HMFO WT, HMFO V367R-W466F, HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367L-T414K-A419Y-A435E (7xHMFO), HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367L-T414K-A419Y-A435E-W466F (8AxHMFO), HMFO I73V-H74Y-G356H-V367R-T414K-A419Y-A435E-W466F (8BxHMFO), using 5.0 mM 5-(hydroxymethyl)furfural as a substrate were carried out at 25 or 40 °C, with shaking at 1000 rpm.

we now developed a new and facile gene shuffling approach to rapidly combine stabilizing mutations in a one-pot reaction

To further tune the 7xHMFO variant towards conversion of HMF into FDCA, we introduced two mutations (V367R and W466F) in the active site that have been shown to be beneficial for FDCA production from HMF [11].

The gene single point variants, the double mutant I73V H74Y, the 8AxHMFO, and the 8BxHMFO were obtained from pET SUMO-HMFO or pBAD SUMO-7xHMFO by whole-plasmid PCR with PfuUltra II Hotstart PCR Master Mix (Agilent).

Therefore, the best multiple mutant was considered to be the 7xHMFO mutant (I73V, H74Y, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) with a $T_{m}^{app}$ of 60.5 °C and $k_{cat}$ and $K_{m}$ values comparable with the wild-type enzyme (Table 1) [8].

success_verification.final_conclusion: 7xHMFO is a highly thermostable scaffold (Tm_app 60.5 °C) with WT-like activity; 8BxHMFO achieves >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C due to improved operational stability, despite lower catalytic efficiency. Golden Gate gene shuffling efficiently combined stabilizing mutations, advancing the FRESCO protocol.
limitations: Trade-off: activity-enhancing mutations reduce thermostability; 8BxHMFO has lower kcat/Km than the double mutant.
Shuffling efficiency was 65%, not 100%.
